title: Claude Code VS Codex VS OpenCode
summary: NeuralNine compares three code generation tools, Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode, across ease of use, model exclusivity, variety, tooling, efficiency, and practical project performance. The analysis includes a hands-on Sudoku project demonstration and covers open-source considerations.
writeup: A live side-by-side comparison of Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, and OpenCode across four dimensions: default permission behavior, terminal UI design, model exclusivity, and model variety. The through-line is that each tool makes a different tradeoff between control and flexibility, and the right choice depends on whether subscription lock-in, model breadth, or out-of-box caution matters most to the developer.
key_points: Claude Code is the only tool that requires explicit approval before every file write or command by default; Codex and OpenCode both auto-execute without prompting unless the user edits the config file.
For terminal UI design, OpenCode ranks first for polish and animated code preview, Claude Code second, and Codex third; all rankings are based on visual quality alone, not model capability.
The Claude Code Max subscription cannot be used with any third-party tool including OpenCode; the OpenAI subscription, by contrast, can be wired into OpenCode via ChatGPT Plus or Pro.

OpenCode supports the widest model range: free built-in models, Google, xAI, OpenRouter, local models, and Anthropic via API key; Codex is locked to GPT models and Claude Code is locked to Claude models.

The presenter judges Opus 4.7 the strongest available model at time of recording, which makes Claude Code the highest-capability choice at that moment, though that ranking is explicitly model-market-dependent and expected to shift.
